senses.definition: Covered with frost or a thin, white layer of ice.
examples: We walked across the rimy fields just before sunrise.
The rimy grass crunched softly under her boots.
On cold winter mornings, the trees were rimy and still.

senses.definition: A former Japanese coin and money unit, equal to one tenth of a sen.
examples: The old shop sign still listed prices in sen and rin.
He kept a small collection of rin from the early twentieth century.
The coin, worth only a rin, had more sentimental than real value.

senses.definition: An Italian given name and surname, equivalent to the English name Reynold.
examples: Rinaldo studied music in Milan before moving abroad.
The novel follows a young knight named Rinaldo on his journey.
She collaborated with Professor Rinaldo on the research project.

senses.definition: A decorative pattern of a flowing stem with leaves or small branches, used in architecture and art.
examples: The ceiling was covered with a delicate rinceau in gold.
A carved rinceau runs along the top of the doorway.
The designer added a stylized rinceau to frame the painting.

senses.definition: Plural of rinceau: several decorative patterns of flowing stems with leaves or small branches.
examples: The walls were divided into panels filled with different rinceaux.
Medieval manuscripts often include colorful rinceaux in the margins.
Stone rinceaux around the windows give the building a rich look.

senses.definition: In Spanish‑influenced place names, a small corner or secluded area, often used in geographic names.
examples: They rented a cabin in El Rincon above the valley.
The trail curves around a rocky rincon before heading north.
We stopped in a sleepy town called Rincon on our way to the coast.

senses.definition: The thick outer skin or hard layer of some fruits, cheese, or bacon.
examples: Peel off the orange rind before you eat the fruit.
The cheese has a strong flavor, especially near the rind.
She cut away the bacon rind to make it less fatty.

senses.definition: The hard or tough outer layer of something, used figuratively.
examples: Under his hard rind, he was actually very kind.
The ancient city still keeps a rind of old walls around its center.
A thin rind of ice covered the lake in early winter.

senses.definition: Having a rind or thick outer skin.
examples: The shop sells both rinded and rindless cheese.
Thick‑rinded fruit can be stored longer.
The rinded variety has a stronger flavor.

senses.definition: A serious viral disease that affected cattle and some other animals, now eradicated.
examples: Rinderpest once caused huge losses in African cattle herds.
The campaign to eliminate rinderpest is considered a major success.
Farmers feared an outbreak of rinderpest in the region.

senses.definition: Plural of rinderpest: several occurrences or types of the cattle disease rinderpest.
examples: Historical records mention several rinderpests in the nineteenth century.
Researchers compared earlier rinderpests with the final outbreak.
The country had survived two major rinderpests by the 1920s.

senses.definition: Having more rind or a thicker rind than something else.
examples: This variety is rindier but keeps longer.
The older cheese is rindier and has a sharper taste.
Rindier fruits can sometimes be harder to peel.

senses.definition: Having the most rind or the thickest rind compared to others.
examples: This is the rindiest cheese on the plate.
Choose the rindiest fruit if you want it to travel well.
The rindiest pieces were left until last.

senses.definition: Present participle of rind: removing or forming a rind on something.
examples: They were rinding the cheese before packing it.
Rinding the bacon makes it easier to slice.
The long drying process is part of rinding the sausages.

senses.definition: A small stream or water channel, especially in dialect use.
examples: A narrow rindle ran behind the cottage.
The children built a dam across the rindle.
After heavy rain, the dry rindle filled quickly.

senses.definition: Without a rind or thick outer skin.
examples: They served a rindless soft cheese with crackers.
Rindless bacon is popular because it is easier to cook.
The recipe calls for rindless slices of lemon.

senses.definition: Plural of rind: outer skins or hard layers of fruits, cheese, or bacon.
examples: Throw the orange rinds into the compost bin.
Cheese rinds can be used to flavor soup.
He cut off the bacon rinds before frying the pieces.

senses.definition: Having a lot of rind or a thick, tough outer layer.
examples: The melon was disappointingly rindy and not very sweet.
Rindy cheese is harder to cut into smooth slices.
These rindy oranges are good for zest but not for juice.

senses.definition: In music, with a sudden increase in volume for a short passage.
examples: The score marks this chord rinforzando.
Play the final notes rinforzando to emphasize the ending.
The violins enter rinforzando, creating a powerful effect.

senses.definition: Describing a note or passage that should be played with a sudden increase in volume.
examples: There is a rinforzando figure in the middle of the phrase.
A rinforzando accent appears on the downbeat.
The rinforzando section leads into the climax.

senses.definition: A small circle of metal or other material worn on the finger as jewellery.
examples: He bought her a gold ring for their anniversary.
She wears a simple silver ring on her left hand.
The wedding ring was a family heirloom.

senses.definition: A circular shape or object.
examples: The children sat in a ring around the teacher.
Smoke rose in a thin ring above the chimney.
She drew a ring on the board to show the orbit.

senses.definition: A special area, often with a border, where boxing, wrestling, or other contests take place.
examples: The fighters entered the ring to loud applause.
She left the boxing ring exhausted but happy.
He hopes to return to the ring next season.

senses.definition: A group of people who work together secretly, often for a dishonest or criminal purpose.
examples: The police broke up a drug ring operating in the city.
He was part of a smuggling ring that crossed the border at night.
They discovered a ring of thieves inside the company.

senses.definition: The sound that a bell or phone makes, or the quality of that sound.
examples: I woke up to the ring of the doorbell.
Her voice has a confident ring.
He heard the ring of metal on stone.

senses.definition: To call someone by phone.
examples: I will ring you later this evening.
She rang her mother to share the news.
Ring me when you get home safely.

senses.definition: To make a clear sound like a bell.
examples: The church bells rang at noon.
Her laughter rang through the empty hall.
Coins rang on the metal tray.

senses.definition: To surround something or someone in the shape of a circle.
examples: Tall trees ring the lake.
Mountains ringed the small town.
They ringed the camp with a simple fence.

senses.definition: Able to be rung, especially of bells.
examples: Only three of the old bells are still ringable.
The tower holds eight ringable bells.
They restored the damaged bell to make it ringable again.

senses.definition: A Maori religious movement in New Zealand that combines Christian and Maori beliefs.
examples: The Ringatu church has a strong presence in some Maori communities.
He wrote his thesis on Ringatu traditions and rituals.
Ringatu worship blends Christian readings with Maori practice.

senses.definition: To remove a ring of bark from around a tree trunk to kill the tree.
examples: Farmers sometimes ringbark unwanted trees to clear land.
They decided to ringbark the old pines instead of cutting them down.
Ringbarking trees can have serious effects on the ecosystem.

senses.definition: The bark removed from a tree by ringbarking, or the area where it has been removed.
examples: The ringbark around the trunk was clearly visible.
Fresh ringbark left the wood exposed.
The ringbark marks showed where the tree had been damaged.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of ringbark.
examples: They ringbarked several trees along the fence line.
The trees had been ringbarked years ago.
Farmers ringbarked the land to prepare it for crops.

senses.definition: A person or tool that ringbarks trees.
examples: The ringbarker moved quickly from tree to tree.
As a ringbarker, he was hired to clear unwanted vegetation.
The company employed several ringbarkers during the project.

senses.definition: The act or practice of removing a ring of bark from trees to kill them.
examples: Ringbarking was once common in land‑clearing practices.
The report criticizes large‑scale ringbarking in the region.
Ringbarking can lead to soil erosion and habitat loss.

senses.definition: Present participle of ringbark.
examples: They were ringbarking trees along the new road.
Ringbarking the old pines will open space for new growth.
She spent the summer ringbarking invasive species.

senses.definition: Third person singular of ringbark.
examples: The farmer ringbarks the trees instead of cutting them down.
If you ringbark a tree, it usually dies within a year.
He ringbarks unwanted saplings each spring.

senses.definition: A duck with a dark ring on its bill, especially the North American ring‑necked duck.
examples: We spotted a ringbill on the lake near the reeds.
The guide pointed out several ringbills among the other ducks.
Ringbills are common winter visitors to this area.

senses.definition: A bird with a ring or band around its neck, especially a ring‑necked pheasant or similar bird.
examples: The hunter saw a ringbird disappear into the long grass.
Ringbirds are often introduced for sport.
We photographed a bright ringbird near the edge of the field.

senses.definition: A type of horse's bit that includes a ring around the lower jaw.
examples: The old saddle and ringbit were stored in the barn.
He preferred a ringbit for better control of strong horses.
The trainer switched to a ringbit during the session.

senses.definition: Plural of ringbit: several horse bits with a ring around the lower jaw.
examples: The tack room contained several old ringbits.
She collected antique ringbits and spurs.
Modern ringbits are designed with safety in mind.

senses.definition: A bolt with a ring attached to its head, used for fastening ropes or chains.
examples: They fixed the boat to a ringbolt on the quay.
The ringbolt pulled free under the strain.
A rusty ringbolt stuck out of the wall.

senses.definition: Plural of ringbolt: several bolts with rings for fastening ropes or chains.
examples: Ringbolts around the deck keep the lines in place.
They replaced the old ringbolts with stronger ones.
Several ringbolts were fixed along the stone pier.

senses.definition: A bony swelling of the pastern or coffin joint in a horse, often causing lameness.
examples: The vet diagnosed the horse with ringbone.
Ringbone can seriously affect a horse's performance.
Proper shoeing may help manage ringbone.

senses.definition: Affected by ringbone, having a bony swelling in the pastern or coffin joint.
examples: A ringboned horse often moves stiffly.
They retired the ringboned mare from competition.
The trainer is cautious about buying ringboned animals.

senses.definition: Plural of ringbone: several cases or areas of bony swelling in horses' pastern or coffin joints.
examples: The clinic treats many ringbones each year.
Old ringbones can cause chronic pain.
They studied ringbones in working horses.

senses.definition: Skill and tactical awareness in the boxing ring.
examples: His ringcraft impressed even experienced trainers.
She won the match through clever ringcraft rather than power.
Good ringcraft can make up for a lack of strength.

senses.definition: The skills needed to handle and present dogs in a show ring.
examples: The club offers classes in ringcraft for new handlers.
Good ringcraft helps a dog look its best in competition.
She teaches ringcraft at the local kennel club.

senses.definition: A large European pigeon with a white band around its neck, also called a wood pigeon.
examples: A ringdove cooed softly in the trees.
The garden is often visited by a pair of ringdoves.
They identified the bird as a ringdove from its call.

senses.definition: Plural of ringdove: several large pigeons with a white band around the neck.
examples: Ringdoves gathered to feed under the oak tree.
The sound of ringdoves filled the quiet park.
We watched the ringdoves circle above the field.

senses.definition: Having one or more rings around it.
examples: Saturn is a ringed planet.
The ringed bird was part of a long‑term study.
A ringed pattern appeared on the old wood.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of ring: to have called by phone.
examples: I ringed her yesterday but she did not answer.
He had already ringed the office when the email arrived.
They ringed us several times about the booking.

senses.definition: In botany, having a gaping or widely open mouth, like certain flowers.
examples: The ringent flowers are pollinated by large bees.
A ringent corolla gives the plant a striking look.
The species is known for its ringent, open‑mouthed blooms.

senses.definition: A person or thing that rings, such as someone who rings bells.
examples: The church ringer arrived early on Sunday.
She worked as a telephone ringer in the call center.
The bell ringer practiced a new pattern.

senses.definition: In informal use, someone who looks very similar to another person.
examples: He is a real ringer for his grandfather.
She found a ringer for the famous actress in the crowd.
The child is a ringer for his older brother.

senses.definition: In sports, a highly skilled player secretly added to a weaker team to give it an advantage, especially against the rules.
examples: They accused the opponents of using a ringer in the final.
The new player turned out to be a ringer from a higher league.
Bringing in a ringer is against the spirit of the game.

senses.definition: Plural of ringer: people who ring bells.
examples: The ringers met every Thursday to practice.
Skilled ringers can handle complex patterns.
Church ringers often volunteer their time.

senses.definition: Plural of ringer: people on a team who are much better than the others, often brought in to gain an unfair advantage.
examples: The league banned teams that relied on ringers.
They suspected the visiting team had a couple of ringers.
Using ringers in a friendly game upset the other players.

senses.definition: A fast team sport played on ice, where players use straight sticks to control a rubber ring and try to score goals.
examples: She has played ringette since she was eight years old.
Ringette is especially popular among girls and women in Canada.
The local arena hosts youth ringette games every weekend.

senses.definition: Plural of ringette: games or teams in the sport of ringette.
examples: The club offers both youth and adult ringettes.
She coaches several ringettes during the winter season.
Local ringettes attract large crowds on weekends.

senses.definition: A fish with a noticeable ring around its eye, used as a common name for several species.
examples: The fisherman caught a small ringeye near the reef.
Ringeye are often found in shallow coastal waters.
They served grilled ringeye at the seaside restaurant.
